vulnerability.title: Disable the use of user namespaces
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the user.max_user_namespaces kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: user.max_user_namespaces = 0 When containers are deployed on the machine, the value should be set to large non-zero value.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Disable the use of user namespaces

triage_decision.reason: User namespaces can be safely disabled by setting user.max_user_namespaces=0; this is a low-risk sysctl tweak that does not affect SSH or critical services.

execution_details.stderr: You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions:FILE_NOT_FOUND

# sysctl settings are defined through files in

# Vendors settings live in /usr/lib/sysctl.d/.
# To override a whole file, create a new file with the same in
# /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there. To override
# only specific settings, add a file with a lexically later
# name in /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there.
#

execution_details.stdout:
#  This file is part of systemd.
#
#  systemd is free software; you can redistribute it and/or modify it
#  under the terms of the GNU Lesser General Public License as published by
#  the Free Software Foundation; either version 2.1 of the License, or
#  (at your option) any later version.

# See sysctl.d(5) and core(5) for documentation.

# To override settings in this file, create a local file in /etc
# (e.g. /etc/sysctl.d/90-override.conf), and put any assignments
# there.

# System Request functionality of the kernel (SYNC)
#
# Use kernel.sysrq = 1 to allow all keys.
# See https://docs.kernel.org/admin-guide/sysrq.html for a list
# of values and keys.
kernel.sysrq = 16

# Append the PID to the core filename
kernel.core_uses_pid = 1

# Source route verification
net.ipv4.conf.default.rp_filter = 2
net.ipv4.conf.*.rp_filter = 2
-net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter

# Do not accept source routing
net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_source_route = 0
net.ipv4.conf.*.accept_source_route = 0
-net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_source_route

# Promote secondary addresses when the primary address is removed
net.ipv4.conf.default.promote_secondaries = 1
net.ipv4.conf.*.promote_secondaries = 1
-net.ipv4.conf.all.promote_secondaries

# ping(8) without CAP_NET_ADMIN and CAP_NET_RAW
# The upper limit is set to 2^31-1. Values greater than that get rejected by
# the kernel because of this definition in linux/include/net/ping.h:
#   #define GID_T_MAX (((gid_t)~0U) >> 1)
# That's not so bad because values between 2^31 and 2^32-1 are reserved on
# systemd-based systems anyway: https://systemd.io/UIDS-GIDS#summary
-net.ipv4.ping_group_range = 0 2147483647

# Fair Queue CoDel packet scheduler to fight bufferbloat
-net.core.default_qdisc = fq_codel

# Enable hard and soft link protection
fs.protected_hardlinks = 1
fs.protected_symlinks = 1

# Enable regular file and FIFO protection
fs.protected_regular = 1
fs.protected_fifos = 1

review_feedback: Review+QA approved but the verification scan failed. Try a different approach.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The fix correctly disables user namespaces by setting user.max_user_namespaces=0 via a sysctl drop-in, but it may impact container workloads that rely on user-namespace support.
concerns: Disabling user namespaces can break rootless containers and orchestration platforms that depend on them.
The change must be coordinated with container management teams to avoid service disruption.
suggested_improvements: Assess the impact on existing container workloads before applying the setting.
Document the change and include a rollback plan in case containers become unavailable.
Consider monitoring the sysctl value to ensure it remains at 0 after reboots.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly disables user namespaces by setting user.max_user_namespaces=0 via a sysctl drop-in, meeting the vulnerability requirement.
concerns: Disabling user namespaces can break rootless containers and orchestration platforms that depend on them.
The change must be coordinated with teams relying on container workloads to avoid service disruption.
No explicit rollback plan or monitoring is described to ensure the setting persists and is visible after reboots.
suggested_improvements: Assess the impact on existing container workloads before applying the setting.
Document a rollback procedure and include it in change management documentation.
Add monitoring or verification steps to ensure user.max_user_namespaces remains 0 after reboots.
Consider alternative approaches if rootless containers are required, such as using a non‑zero limit with explicit scoping.
